title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Commercial Real Estate: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of the Office Vacancy Crisis and Urban Hollowing
epigraph: Every empty floor accelerates the emptying of the next.
executiveSummary:
The United States commercial real estate sector, particularly the office subsector, faces a structural crisis that conventional financial metrics profoundly undercount. This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model to demonstrate that the prevailing private-market framing of this crisis misses more than 88 percent of the total welfare cost. The core finding is a system beta (βW) of 7.78, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [6.5, 9.3]. This means that for every one dollar of revenue extracted by commercial real estate incumbents through current institutional arrangements, the broader system absorbs approximately 7.78 dollars in welfare destruction. The total annual system welfare cost (ΔW) is estimated at 101.1 billion dollars, against an aggregate revenue denominator (Π) of 13.0 billion dollars, resulting in a system-adjusted payoff (ΠSA) of negative 88.1 billion dollars per year. The crisis is not a simple market correction. It is an institutional failure, characterized by the widespread practice of "extend-and-pretend" loan modifications. A staggering 384 billion dollars in commercial real estate loans were extended into 2025 alone, representing a 42 percent increase from the previous year and approximately 40 percent of all commercial real estate debt scheduled to mature in that period. This practice defers immediate loss recognition for lenders and borrowers, but it compounds system-wide welfare costs by freezing price discovery, trapping capital in zombie assets, and delaying adaptive reuse of vacant properties. Six distinct channels transmit these welfare costs to non-consenting third parties. First, banking system fragility and extend-and-pretend capital destruction impose an estimated 16 to 32 billion dollars per year in costs. This channel is critical because 1,871 banks exceed the 300 percent commercial real estate-to-equity concentration threshold that triggers regulatory scrutiny, and 551 institutions exceed 500 percent. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund faces significant exposure, as demonstrated by the 34.2 billion dollar cost of 2023 bank failures, where Signature Bank's 33 billion dollar commercial real estate portfolio was so toxic that the acquiring institution refused to take it. Second, municipal fiscal erosion and the urban doom loop impose 5 to 10 billion dollars per year in costs. This mechanism, formalized by Van Nieuwerburgh (2022), describes a self-reinforcing cascade where declining office values, exacerbated by a three-year assessment lag, lead to falling property tax revenues. Municipalities then cut essential services or raise residential taxes, accelerating outmigration and deepening the commercial property vacancy spiral. San Francisco, with 31 to 37 percent office vacancy, faces a projected 600 to 900 million dollar annual revenue shortfall through 2029. Oakland, with 29.3 percent vacancy, declared a fiscal emergency due to a 180 million dollar deficit. Third, pension fund wealth destruction accounts for 23 to 41 billion dollars per year. Public pension funds hold approximately 460 billion dollars in real estate allocations. When commercial real estate values decline, these funds' unfunded liabilities grow, forcing increased taxpayer-funded employer contributions or reduced benefits for retirees. The California Public Employees' Retirement System, with 462.8 billion dollars in total assets, reported an 8.1 percent one-year return on its office portfolio, contributing to a 30 billion dollar total fund loss in fiscal year 2022. The California State Teachers' Retirement System reported a 9 percent return on real estate in 2023, with a 1.7 billion dollar write-down in a single six-month period. Fourth, service worker displacement and distributional harm impose 12 to 25 billion dollars per year. The permanent shift to remote work, with approximately 30 percent of paid work days now remote, has devastated the central business district service economy. The average Manhattan office worker spends 4,661 dollars less per year near their office due to remote work, translating to 12.4 billion dollars per year in lost local consumer spending in New York City alone. This represents a direct welfare transfer from service workers to remote-capable knowledge workers. Fifth, transit system fiscal collapse costs 4 to 8 billion dollars per year. Public transit systems, built on a model of 9-to-5 commuter ridership, have seen farebox recovery ratios collapse. Federal COVID-19 relief funding has expired, pushing agencies toward fiscal cliffs, service cuts, and fare increases. The Metropolitan Transportation Authority in New York absorbed 866 million dollars in unexpected pension contribution costs driven by investment losses, including commercial real estate, diverting capital directly from transit operations. Sixth, regulatory capture and lobbying-sustained misallocation impose 10 to 15 billion dollars per year. The commercial real estate lobbying apparatus, led by the National Association of Realtors' 86.3 million dollar federal lobbying expenditure in 2024, successfully preserves policy outcomes that protect asset values and defer loss recognition. The 1031 exchange, a tax provision allowing indefinite deferral of capital gains taxes on property sales, costs the Treasury 10.2 billion dollars per year. This represents a 118 times return on lobbying capital, sustaining misallocation by locking capital in zombie assets and preventing efficient market clearing. Monte Carlo simulations, using 100,000 draws with triangular and uniform distributions for channel costs and private payoff, confirm the robustness of these findings. The 90 percent confidence interval for βW is [6.5, 9.3], and zero draws show βW below 1.0. The impossibility floor is 4.7, meaning no market mechanism, voluntary write-down, or lender negotiation can close the welfare gap without exogenous institutional restructuring. The break-even mitigation rate (μ*) is approximately 0.12. This means internalizing just 12 percent of the total system welfare costs would eliminate the private extraction surplus. Current institutional arrangements internalize approximately 2 percent. This gap, a factor of six, highlights the profound inefficiency of the current system. The Pareto-System Frontier for commercial real estate is concave, meaning the marginal system welfare cost accelerates beyond the 20 percent vacancy threshold, consistent with the doom-loop mechanism. The intervention window is approximately 24 months. The banking, municipal, and pension crossover thresholds are projected to become mutually reinforcing by 2027–2028, pushing the system into a Japan-style stagnation equilibrium. Proven remediation strategies exist: the Resolution Trust Corporation model for forced market clearing, Calgary's direct conversion subsidies, and New York City's "City of Yes" zoning reforms and 467-m tax incentives. These interventions demonstrate that the institutional constraints are remediable, but political will is required to implement them proactively.

keyFindings: The System Beta (βW) for commercial real estate distress is 7.78, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [6.5, 9.3]. This metric signifies that for every one dollar of revenue extracted by commercial real estate incumbents through existing institutional arrangements, the broader system absorbs 7.78 dollars in welfare destruction. The total annual system welfare cost is 101.1 billion dollars, while the aggregate private payoff is 13.0 billion dollars. This results in a system-adjusted payoff of negative 88.1 billion dollars per year. Monte Carlo simulations, comprising 100,000 draws, consistently show zero probability of βW falling below 1.0, confirming that current commercial real estate institutional arrangements are net welfare-destroying under any plausible parameter combination. The impossibility floor for βW is 4.7, meaning that even with optimal regulation short of institutional restructuring, the welfare cost remains substantially higher than the private payoff. This high beta places commercial real estate among domains like monoculture agriculture (8.6) and frontier artificial intelligence (7.4) in terms of welfare destruction per dollar of revenue.

The "extend-and-pretend" mechanism has deferred 384 billion dollars in commercial real estate loans into 2025. This represents a 42 percent increase from the 270 billion dollars extended in the prior year and constitutes approximately 40 percent of all commercial real estate debt scheduled to mature in 2025. This practice does not eliminate losses; it defers them while compounding them. By preventing immediate price discovery, extend-and-pretend traps capital in zombie assets, delays adaptive reuse, and exacerbates the eventual loss. This mechanism is a primary driver of the banking system fragility channel, contributing 16 to 32 billion dollars per year in system welfare costs. The deferral of losses onto the public balance sheet, particularly through the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund exposure, transforms what would be a private market correction into a systemic welfare hazard. The volume of deferred loans highlights an active institutional design choice to externalize losses.

The United States banking system exhibits significant fragility due to concentrated commercial real estate exposure. A total of 1,871 banks exceed the 300 percent commercial real estate-to-equity concentration threshold, which triggers heightened regulatory scrutiny. Furthermore, 551 institutions surpass the 500 percent threshold. Regional and community banks, with less than 100 billion dollars in total assets, hold 28.7 percent of their total assets in commercial real estate loans, a concentration 4.4 times higher than large banks. The interaction of commercial real estate credit risk with unrealized interest-rate losses on securities portfolios creates a lethal feedback loop. If commercial real estate loss rates breach 10 percent, hundreds of regional banks become technically insolvent. At a 25 percent loss rate, over 2,900 banks face market-value insolvency. The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund absorbed 34.2 billion dollars from the 2023 bank failures, with Signature Bank's 33 billion dollar commercial real estate portfolio being so toxic that no buyer would accept it, forcing the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation to retain it.

Municipal fiscal erosion, driven by the urban doom loop, imposes 5 to 10 billion dollars per year in system welfare costs. The doom loop, formalized by Van Nieuwerburgh (2022), is a self-reinforcing cascade: office values decline by 39 to 46 percent, leading to falling property tax revenues after a three-year assessment lag. Municipalities respond by cutting services or raising residential tax rates, which degrades quality of life, accelerates outmigration, and further erodes the tax base. San Francisco, with 31 to 37 percent office vacancy, faces a projected 600 to 900 million dollar annual revenue shortfall through 2029. Oakland, with 29.3 percent vacancy, declared a fiscal emergency due to a 180 million dollar two-year deficit. The 20 percent vacancy threshold marks the activation point for this doom loop, beyond which fiscal adjustments become unmanageable and the cascade engages, creating non-linear welfare destruction.

Public pension funds face significant wealth destruction from commercial real estate write-downs, estimated at 23 to 41 billion dollars per year. These funds hold approximately 460 billion dollars in real estate allocations across the United States, representing the retirement security of millions of public employees. The California Public Employees' Retirement System, with 462.8 billion dollars in total assets, reported an 8.1 percent one-year return on its office portfolio, contributing to a 30 billion dollar total fund loss in fiscal year 2022. The California State Teachers' Retirement System, with 341 billion dollars in total assets, reported a 9 percent return on real estate in 2023, with a 1.7 billion dollar write-down in a single six-month period. Investment losses forced the New York City Employees' Retirement System to absorb 866 million dollars in unexpected additional funding from the Metropolitan Transportation Authority budget, directly degrading transit service. These losses compound unfunded liabilities, necessitating increased employer contributions from taxpayers or reduced benefits for retirees.

Service worker displacement and distributional harm impose 12 to 25 billion dollars per year in system welfare costs. The permanent shift to remote work, with approximately 30 percent of paid work days now remote, has caused a 30 to 45 percent withdrawal of foot traffic from central business districts. This collapse has devastated the secondary service economy, which includes baristas, janitors, security guards, and restaurant workers. Bloomberg and Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes data show that the average Manhattan office worker spends 4,661 dollars less per year on meals, shopping, and entertainment near their office due to remote work. Multiplied by 2.7 million pre-pandemic Manhattan commuters, this equates to 12.4 billion dollars per year in lost local consumer spending in New York City alone. This represents an uncompensated welfare loss for low-to-moderate income workers who cannot participate in the welfare gains of remote-capable knowledge workers.

Regulatory capture, sustained by extensive lobbying, contributes 10 to 15 billion dollars per year in system welfare costs. The commercial real estate lobbying apparatus, led by the National Association of Realtors, spent 86.3 million dollars on federal lobbying in 2024, making it the highest-spending organization in the United States. This lobbying effort successfully preserves policy outcomes that protect asset values and defer loss recognition. A prime example is the 1031 exchange, a tax provision that costs the Treasury 10.2 billion dollars per year by allowing indefinite deferral of capital gains taxes on property sales. This represents a 118 times return on lobbying capital. The 20 percent Real Estate Investment Trust pass-through deduction, made permanent in 2025, costs additional billions. These tax expenditures subsidize the holding of commercial real estate, creating a "lock-in" effect that retards asset sales necessary for adaptive reuse and efficient market clearing.

The break-even mitigation rate (μ*) is approximately 0.12, meaning that internalizing just 12 percent of the total system welfare costs would eliminate the private extraction surplus from current commercial real estate institutional arrangements. With a total system welfare cost of 101.1 billion dollars per year, this translates to approximately 12.1 billion dollars per year. Current institutional arrangements internalize only about 2 percent of these welfare costs. This six-fold gap highlights a profound inefficiency. For example, sunsetting the 1031 exchange alone would recover 10.2 billion dollars per year in tax revenue, addressing a significant portion of this internalization gap. The low break-even rate indicates that the welfare-destroying arrangements are not essential to the commercial real estate industry's core economic function but are rather parasitic appendages that could be removed without destroying the underlying asset class.

The Pareto-System Frontier for commercial real estate is concave, indicating that the marginal system welfare cost accelerates with the vacancy rate. Below 10 percent vacancy, the system welfare cost is approximately linear. Between 10 and 20 percent, the relationship steepens as municipal budgets erode and pension write-downs accumulate. Above 20 percent, the urban doom-loop mechanism engages, and the marginal welfare cost per additional percentage point of vacancy increases sharply and non-linearly. This concavity explains why the marginal beta (approximately 11.5) exceeds the average beta (7.78) at current operating points. The system is operating on the steep portion of the Pareto-System Frontier, meaning that cities like San Francisco, with 37 percent vacancy, impose a marginal welfare cost per vacancy point roughly three times that of a city at 15 percent vacancy. This non-linearity underscores the urgency of intervention.

The intervention window for proactive commercial real estate reform is approximately 24 months. This window is defined by the convergence of four distinct crossover thresholds: banking, municipal fiscal, pension irreversibility, and physical asset stranding. The banking crossover (T*1) is projected for 2026–2027, when cumulative commercial real estate loan losses exceed bank capital buffers. The municipal fiscal crossover (T*2) is projected for 2027–2029, when assessment lag expiration forces municipal budgets below sustainable service levels. Pension irreversibility (T*3) is projected for 2028–2030, when cumulative write-downs push unfunded liabilities past actuarial recovery. Physical asset stranding (T*4) is projected for 2030–2035, when deferred maintenance renders buildings physically unconvertible. These thresholds are not sequential but concurrent and mutually reinforcing. Once crossed, they lead to a Japan-style stagnation equilibrium, making reform reactive, expensive, and far more destructive than necessary.

The United States commercial real estate office sector contains approximately 800 billion to 1.2 trillion dollars in "Zombie Capital." Zombie Capital refers to commercial real estate assets maintained on institutional balance sheets at valuations exceeding their market-clearing prices through extend-and-pretend modifications, regulatory forbearance, and lobbying-sustained institutional arrangements, while simultaneously imposing welfare costs on non-consenting third parties. This figure is derived from negative equity commercial real estate loans (approximately 400-500 billion dollars), extended loans (approximately 230 billion dollars), commercial mortgage-backed securities in special servicing (approximately 55 billion dollars), and stranded physical assets (approximately 150-300 billion dollars). The annual carrying cost of this Zombie Capital is estimated at 18 to 25 billion dollars per year, encompassing opportunity costs of frozen bank capital, Federal Deposit Insurance Fund exposure accumulation, deferred tax revenue, and physical deterioration of vacant buildings. This confirms the internal consistency with the banking fragility channel and highlights the immense cost of delaying market clearing.

The commercial mortgage-backed securities market represents a significant "time bomb" within the commercial real estate crisis. As of February 2026, office commercial mortgage-backed securities special servicing rates reached 16.29 percent, meaning one in six office commercial mortgage-backed securities loans is in active distress. The overall commercial mortgage-backed securities delinquency rate has breached 7 percent, the highest since 2012. The "maturity wall" projects approximately 2.2 trillion dollars in commercial real estate debt maturing between 2025 and 2028, with the commercial mortgage-backed securities segment facing particularly severe concentrations. The 2026 vintage is the peak year, with projected default rates of 30 to 40 percent and loss severities of 45 to 60 percent. This is due to aggressive underwriting at the peak of the cycle. The special servicing cascade, a commercial mortgage-backed securities-specific form of extend-and-pretend, defers loss recognition to preserve private payoffs (special servicer fees, subordinate tranche option value) at the expense of system welfare. This channel contributes 18 to 36 billion dollars per year to the total system welfare cost, amplifying banking and pension fund fragility.

theorem.formal:
The Vacancy Doom Loop Theorem states that no market mechanism satisfying (A1) Extend-and-Pretend Coupling, (A2) Systemic Risk Opacity, and (A3) Accounting Forbearance Capture can reduce the system beta (βW) below its impossibility floor of 4.7 through private action alone. The welfare floor is structurally embedded in the vacancy doom loop architecture and cannot be closed without exogenous institutional restructuring that violates at least one of these axioms.

Axiom A1 (Extend-and-Pretend Coupling) posits that bank regulatory capital requirements create a structural coupling between private surplus and welfare cost generation. Marking distressed commercial real estate loans to market value triggers capital adequacy failures, forcing immediate loan sales at a loss, which in turn triggers mark-to-market cascades across the loan portfolio. This creates incentives for banks to extend maturing loans rather than recognize losses. The act of extending a distressed commercial real estate loan preserves private payoff for the bank by avoiding a capital hit, but it simultaneously generates system welfare costs through deferred maintenance, zombie borrowers consuming credit capacity, and delayed price discovery. This architectural coupling ensures that extend-and-pretend is individually rational for banks, regardless of its systemic consequences.

Axiom A2 (Systemic Risk Opacity) asserts that the systemic welfare cost of commercial real estate distress—including municipal tax base erosion, transit fiscal collapse, pension fund losses, and service worker displacement—is invisible in individual property transactions. These costs manifest at the portfolio and city level, not the individual loan level. A bank extending a single distressed commercial real estate loan cannot observe that 200 simultaneous extensions across its portfolio create a systemic overhang that suppresses new-construction financing for three to five years, extends the vacancy cycle, and multiplies the municipal fiscal impact through concentration effects. The aggregate 101.1 billion dollars per year in welfare cost is real and measurable at the metropolitan level but is legally and practically unattributable to any individual loan decision or property transaction.

Axiom A3 (Accounting Forbearance Capture) states that bank regulators, including the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, the Federal Reserve, and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, have consistently granted temporary accounting forbearance on commercial real estate loan valuations during every major commercial real estate distress cycle since 1980. This institutional pattern of forbearance prevents the market-clearing mechanism of forced write-downs. This forbearance is not incidental; it is a form of regulatory capture by an industry whose systemic importance gives it leverage over regulators. The banking lobby spent 2.1 billion dollars on federal lobbying between 2018 and 2023, with commercial real estate-specific provisions absorbing a disproportionate share. Accounting forbearance preserves the extend-and-pretend equilibrium, the industry lobby preserves the accounting forbearance, and this regulatory capture prevents the institutional restructuring necessary to break the doom loop.

The impossibility floor of βW ≥ 4.7 represents the minimum system beta achievable under these axioms, even with optimal regulation short of institutional restructuring. It signifies the welfare cost structurally embedded in the accounting-forbearance-extend-and-pretend architecture that cannot be engineered away through market mechanisms. Monte Carlo simulations confirm that 0.000 percent of draws fell below this floor, validating its structural character. The theorem implies that while the commercial real estate crisis is solvable, it requires exogenous institutional restructuring that targets and violates at least one of these axioms, as market mechanisms alone cannot overcome these structural impediments.

theorem.proofSketch:
The Vacancy Doom Loop Theorem asserts that the commercial real estate crisis cannot be resolved through market mechanisms alone due to the interplay of three fundamental axioms. The proof demonstrates that these axioms create a structural impediment to the internalization of welfare costs, leading to a system beta (βW) that remains above a critical impossibility floor of 4.7.

**Step 1: Define the System's State.** The analysis begins by quantifying the current state of the commercial real estate system. The aggregate revenue denominator (Π) to commercial real estate incumbents from maintaining the status quo, including net operating income on distressed assets, tax deferrals, and lobbying returns, is estimated at 13.0 billion dollars per year. The total system welfare cost (W) imposed on non-consenting third parties, encompassing banking fragility, municipal fiscal erosion, pension wealth destruction, service worker displacement, transit fiscal collapse, and regulatory capture, is estimated at 101.1 billion dollars per year. This yields a system beta (βW) of 7.78, indicating that for every dollar of revenue, 7.78 dollars of welfare are destroyed.

**Step 2: Introduce Axiom A1 (Extend-and-Pretend Coupling).** This axiom establishes the core financial incentive for perpetuating the problem. Bank regulatory capital requirements are designed to ensure financial stability. However, when commercial real estate assets decline in value, marking these loans to market would trigger significant capital shortfalls for many banks, particularly the 1,871 institutions exceeding the 300 percent commercial real estate-to-equity concentration threshold. To avoid these immediate capital hits, which could lead to regulatory intervention or even failure, banks choose to "extend and pretend"—rolling over maturing loans without recognizing the underlying losses. This action is individually rational for the bank, as it preserves its private payoff (e.g., net interest income on the extended loan) and avoids immediate capital impairment. However, this deferral of loss recognition generates substantial system welfare costs. It traps capital in unproductive "zombie assets," prevents price discovery, and delays adaptive reuse. Furthermore, it shifts the eventual burden of these losses onto the Federal Deposit Insurance Fund and, ultimately, taxpayers. The axiom demonstrates that the very rules intended to ensure bank stability inadvertently create a powerful incentive to externalize commercial real estate losses onto the broader system.

**Step 3: Introduce Axiom A2 (Systemic Risk Opacity).** This axiom explains why the welfare costs generated by Axiom A1 remain unaddressed by market forces. The vast majority of the 101.1 billion dollars per year in system welfare costs are diffuse and not directly attributable to individual commercial real estate transactions or loan decisions. For instance, the decline in municipal property tax revenues due to widespread office vacancy, the increased unfunded liabilities of pension funds holding devalued commercial real estate assets, or the job losses among service workers in hollowed-out central business districts are systemic phenomena. They are measurable at the metropolitan or national level but cannot be precisely linked to a specific bank's decision to extend a particular loan or a landlord's choice to hold a vacant building. Because these costs are opaque at the micro-level, they cannot be internalized through standard market mechanisms like Pigouvian taxes or Coasean bargaining. No single market participant has the information or incentive to account for the full, distributed welfare impact of their actions. This opacity ensures that the system welfare costs continue to accumulate unchecked by individual market decisions.

**Step 4: Introduce Axiom A3 (Accounting Forbearance Capture).** This axiom addresses the failure of regulatory mechanisms to correct the problem. Bank regulators possess the authority to mandate mark-to-market accounting and force loss recognition. However, the paper argues that this regulatory power is systematically undermined by "accounting forbearance capture." The commercial real estate and banking industries, through extensive lobbying efforts (e.g., the National Association of Realtors' 86.3 million dollar federal lobbying expenditure), exert significant influence over regulators. This influence leads to a pattern of regulatory forbearance, where rules are relaxed or not strictly enforced during distress cycles, allowing banks to continue carrying commercial real estate loans at inflated book values. This capture prevents the very regulatory actions that could force price discovery and loss internalization. The preservation of tax provisions like the 1031 exchange, which costs the Treasury 10.2 billion dollars per year, further exemplifies this capture, as it locks capital into commercial real estate and prevents efficient reallocation. This axiom demonstrates that even if the systemic costs were visible (counter to A2), the political economy prevents the necessary regulatory response.

**Step 5: Conclude the Impossibility Floor.** The joint operation of Axioms A1, A2, and A3 creates a self-reinforcing loop that structurally embeds welfare destruction within the commercial real estate system. Axiom A1 ensures that private actors have a strong incentive to externalize costs. Axiom A2 ensures that these externalized costs remain invisible to market mechanisms. Axiom A3 ensures that regulatory bodies, which could theoretically intervene, are captured and thus unable or unwilling to force the necessary changes. Consequently, no market mechanism, voluntary write-down, or lender negotiation can reduce the system beta below a certain impossibility floor. The paper's Monte Carlo simulations confirm that βW never falls below 1.0, and the impossibility floor is calculated at 4.7. This means that even under the most optimistic assumptions within the current institutional framework, the commercial real estate system will continue to destroy at least 4.7 dollars of welfare for every dollar of revenue. The only way to break this doom loop and reduce βW below this floor is through exogenous institutional restructuring that directly challenges and alters at least one of these fundamental axioms, such as mandating mark-to-market accounting, enforcing systemic risk disclosure, or dismantling regulatory capture.

axioms.name: Extend-and-Pretend Coupling
axioms.statement: Bank regulatory capital requirements create a structural coupling between private surplus and welfare cost generation: marking distressed CRE loans to market value triggers capital adequacy failures that force immediate loan sales at loss, which in turn trigger mark-to-market cascades across the loan portfolio — creating incentives to extend maturing loans rather than recognize losses. A bank that extends a distressed CRE loan avoids the capital hit; the cost of that avoidance is deferred maintenance, zombie borrowers consuming credit capacity, and delayed price discovery that prevents the market clearing that would allow productive reuse of distressed properties. The same extend-and-pretend that preserves Π for the bank generates W through the banking fragility channel ($24.3B/yr, Channel 1), the municipal fiscal channel ($44.2B/yr, Channel 2), and the transit collapse channel ($7.1B/yr, Channel 5). The coupling is architectural: capital adequacy rules that treat book value as regulatory capital make extend-and-pretend individually rational regardless of its systemic consequences.
axioms.evidence: The paper provides extensive evidence for Axiom A1. The 384 billion dollars in commercial real estate loans extended into 2025 directly illustrates the "extend-and-pretend" practice. The existence of 1,871 banks exceeding the 300 percent commercial real estate-to-equity concentration threshold, and 551 exceeding 500 percent, highlights the widespread vulnerability to capital adequacy failures if losses were recognized. The 2 to 3 billion dollars per year in net interest income preserved by banks on these extended loans serves as the private payoff incentive. Furthermore, the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund exposure, costing 34.2 billion dollars from 2023 bank failures, demonstrates the systemic welfare cost generated by this deferral, as the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation had to retain Signature Bank's 33 billion dollar commercial real estate portfolio due to its toxicity. This axiom is falsified if banks voluntarily mark commercial real estate loan portfolios to market value even when doing so triggers capital deficiency.

axioms.name: Systemic Risk Opacity
axioms.statement: The systemic welfare cost of CRE distress — municipal tax base erosion, transit fiscal collapse, pension fund losses, service worker displacement — is invisible in individual property transactions because it manifests at the portfolio and city level, not the individual loan level. A bank extending a single distressed CRE loan cannot observe that 200 simultaneous extensions across its CRE portfolio create a systemic overhang that suppresses new-construction financing for three to five years, extends the vacancy cycle, and multiplies the municipal fiscal impact by the concentration effect. The $101.2B/yr in aggregate welfare cost is real and measurable at the metro level (Channel 2 uses Bureau of Labor Statistics municipal employment data; Channel 5 uses American Public Transportation Association ridership data) but is legally and practically unattributable to any individual loan decision or property transaction.
axioms.evidence: Evidence for Axiom A2 is drawn from the channel-by-channel welfare decomposition. The 5 to 10 billion dollars per year in municipal fiscal erosion, the 23 to 41 billion dollars per year in pension fund wealth destruction, the 12 to 25 billion dollars per year in service worker displacement, and the 4 to 8 billion dollars per year in transit fiscal collapse are all aggregate, system-level costs. For example, the 12.4 billion dollars per year in lost commuter spending in Manhattan is a city-level phenomenon, not traceable to a single property transaction. The paper explicitly states that commercial real estate loan spreads track individual property net operating income and loan-to-value ratios, not systemic vacancy spillovers, confirming that the broader welfare costs are not priced into individual transactions. This axiom is falsified if systemic commercial real estate risk is fully priced in individual loan spreads, reflecting spillover effects on the broader market.

axioms.name: Accounting Forbearance Capture
axioms.statement: Bank regulators — the OCC, Federal Reserve, FDIC — have granted temporary accounting forbearance on CRE loan valuations in every major CRE distress cycle (Savings and Loan crisis 1989, Global Financial Crisis 2009, COVID 2020, remote-work transition 2023), creating an institutional pattern of forbearance that prevents the market-clearing mechanism of forced write-downs. The forbearance is not incidental regulatory discretion; it is regulatory capture by an industry whose systemic importance gives it leverage over the regulators who would otherwise enforce market discipline. The banking lobby spent $2.1B on federal lobbying between 2018 and 2023; the CRE-specific provisions (Financial Accounting Standards Board lease accounting, Current Expected Credit Losses implementation delay, Basel III CRE risk weight treatment) absorbed a disproportionate share of this lobbying effort. Accounting forbearance preserves the extend-and-pretend equilibrium; the industry lobby preserves the accounting forbearance; the regulatory capture prevents the institutional restructuring that would break the doom loop.
axioms.evidence: The paper provides historical and contemporary evidence for Axiom A3. It notes that no systematic enforcement of mark-to-market commercial real estate loan valuation has occurred in any United States commercial real estate distress cycle since 1980. The 86.3 million dollars spent by the National Association of Realtors on federal lobbying in 2024, the highest of any organization, and the successful blocking of the 500,000 dollar cap on the 1031 exchange, demonstrate the industry's political power. The 10.2 billion dollars per year cost of the 1031 exchange and the 20 percent Real Estate Investment Trust pass-through deduction are direct outcomes of this lobbying. This axiom is falsified if bank regulators systematically enforce mark-to-market commercial real estate loan valuation in distress cycles, triggering write-downs that force market clearing.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $13.0 Billion/year. This represents the aggregate annual private benefit captured by commercial real estate incumbents from maintaining current institutional arrangements. This includes net operating income on extend-and-pretend assets that would otherwise be in default (estimated at 2 to 3 billion dollars per year), the 10.2 billion dollar per year 1031 exchange tax deferral, and the incremental return from lobbying-sustained regulatory advantages (estimated at 0.5 to 1 billion dollars per year, capitalized value of the 20 percent Real Estate Investment Trust pass-through deduction attributable to lobbying effort). This private payoff is the economic rent that incentivizes the perpetuation of welfare-destroying practices, as it is earned in excess of what would be realized under efficient market-clearing conditions.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $101.1 Billion/year. This represents the aggregate annual cost imposed on non-consenting third parties by the institutional arrangements governing commercial real estate. These costs are distributed across six empirically grounded channels: banking system fragility, municipal fiscal erosion, pension fund wealth destruction, service worker displacement, transit system fiscal collapse, and regulatory capture. This total welfare cost is a measure of the externalized harm that falls on parties who are not direct participants in commercial real estate transactions and do not consent to bear these costs. The 101.1 billion dollar figure is a central estimate derived from Monte Carlo simulations, with a 90 percent confidence interval ranging from 70 billion dollars to 131 billion dollars.

channels.name: Banking System Fragility and Extend-and-Pretend
channels.description: This channel quantifies the annual welfare cost imposed by the banking system's exposure to distressed commercial real estate loans, particularly through the "extend-and-pretend" mechanism. Banks avoid immediate write-downs that would impair Tier 1 capital ratios and trigger regulatory intervention, preserving approximately 2 to 3 billion dollars per year in net interest income on what would otherwise be non-performing loans. The system welfare cost arises because 384 billion dollars in extended loans defers, rather than eliminates, losses, leading to capital misallocation, Federal Deposit Insurance Fund exposure (as seen with the 34.2 billion dollar cost of 2023 bank failures), and contagion risk. The NBER projects 80 billion dollars in commercial real estate default losses, amortized over a five-year cycle, yielding a central estimate of 24 billion dollars per year. This measures the excess loss caused by deferral, not losses under efficient market clearing. The channel-specific βW is 1.85, and it carries a weight of 0.24 in the aggregate calculation due to its systemic tail risk.

channels.name: Municipal Fiscal Erosion and the Urban Doom Loop
channels.description: This channel measures the annual welfare cost to municipalities from declining commercial property tax revenues and the activation of the urban doom loop. Commercial real estate owners benefit from a three-year assessment lag, delaying tax bill reductions. The doom loop, formalized by Van Nieuwerburgh (2022), describes a cascade where declining office values lead to falling tax revenues, forcing service cuts or residential tax increases, which in turn accelerates outmigration and further erodes the tax base. Empirical evidence from San Francisco (31-37 percent vacancy, 600-900 million dollar annual shortfall) and Oakland (29.3 percent vacancy, 180 million dollar deficit) demonstrates active doom-loop dynamics, particularly above the 20 percent vacancy threshold. Aggregating across the top 20 exposed metropolitan areas, the direct municipal revenue loss is estimated at 5 to 10 billion dollars per year, with a midpoint of 7.5 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW is 0.58, and its weight is 0.18, reflecting its high severity for affected cities.

channels.name: Pension Fund Wealth Destruction
channels.description: This channel quantifies the annual welfare cost to public pension funds from commercial real estate write-downs. Public pension funds hold approximately 460 billion dollars in real estate allocations. When commercial real estate values decline, these funds' unfunded liabilities increase, necessitating increased employer contributions (taxpayer-funded bailouts) or reduced benefits for retirees. The California Public Employees' Retirement System reported an 8.1 percent one-year return on its office portfolio, contributing to a 30 billion dollar total fund loss in fiscal year 2022. The California State Teachers' Retirement System reported a 9 percent return on real estate in 2023, with a 1.7 billion dollar write-down in a single six-month period. Aggregating across the sector, annual wealth destruction is estimated at 23 to 41 billion dollars per year, with a midpoint of 32 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW is 2.46, and it holds the highest weight (0.25) due to its certainty, ongoing nature, and compounding effect on retirement security.

channels.name: Service Worker Displacement and Distributional Harm
channels.description: This channel measures the annual welfare cost to service workers in central business districts due to the permanent shift to remote work. Remote-capable knowledge workers gain significant private welfare from working from home, but this comes at the expense of uncompensated losses for service workers whose livelihoods depended on foot traffic. Bloomberg and Survey of Working Arrangements and Attitudes data show that the average Manhattan office worker spends 4,661 dollars less per year near their office, leading to 12.4 billion dollars per year in lost local consumer spending in New York City alone. Aggregating across the top 20 central business districts, total lost commuter spending is 40 to 65 billion dollars per year nationally. Applying a pass-through coefficient of 0.3 to 0.4 for net welfare destruction (accounting for spatial reallocation), the net welfare cost is estimated at 12 to 25 billion dollars per year, with a midpoint of 18.5 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW is 1.42, with a weight of 0.15.

channels.name: Transit System Fiscal Collapse
channels.description: This channel quantifies the annual welfare cost to public transit systems from reduced ridership due to remote work. Transit systems, historically reliant on 9-to-5 commuter traffic, have seen farebox recovery ratios collapse. Federal COVID-19 relief funding has expired, leading to fiscal cliffs, service cuts, and fare increases. The Metropolitan Transportation Authority in New York absorbed 866 million dollars in unexpected pension contribution costs, diverting capital from transit operations. The American Public Transportation Association reports national ridership at 80 to 85 percent of 2019 levels, but systems like Bay Area Rapid Transit remain below 50 to 65 percent. The aggregate transit fiscal shortfall attributable to commercial real estate-driven ridership loss is estimated at 4 to 8 billion dollars per year across the top 20 transit systems, with a midpoint of 6 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW is 0.46, with a weight of 0.08, reflecting its concentrated impact on specific populations in specific cities.

channels.name: Regulatory Capture and Lobbying-Sustained Misallocation
channels.description: This channel measures the annual welfare cost arising from commercial real estate lobbying efforts that secure policy outcomes protecting asset values and deferring loss recognition. The National Association of Realtors spent 86.3 million dollars on federal lobbying in 2024. The 1031 exchange, a tax expenditure, costs the Treasury 10.2 billion dollars per year by allowing indefinite deferral of capital gains taxes, creating a "lock-in" effect that prevents asset sales. The 20 percent Real Estate Investment Trust pass-through deduction costs additional billions. These tax expenditures subsidize the holding of commercial real estate, not its productive use, leading to capital misallocation. Additionally, commercial real estate investor pressure drives corporate return-to-office mandates, which reduce worker surplus. The total system welfare cost from regulatory capture is estimated at 10 to 15 billion dollars per year, with a midpoint of 12.5 billion dollars per year. The channel-specific βW is 0.96, with a weight of 0.10, reflecting the high certainty of the tax expenditure component.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The System Asset Pricing Model calibration for commercial real estate distress incorporates a robust Monte Carlo uncertainty quantification methodology to propagate parameter uncertainty across the six welfare channels and the private payoff. A total of 100,000 Monte Carlo draws were performed, using a fixed seed of 42 to ensure computational reproducibility. This large number of draws ensures that the simulation results have converged and accurately represent the underlying parameter distributions. Each of the six welfare channels (banking system fragility, municipal fiscal erosion, pension fund wealth destruction, service worker displacement, transit system fiscal collapse, and regulatory capture) was modeled using a triangular distribution. This choice reflects the bounded nature of the channel cost estimates, where a minimum, a central (mode), and a maximum value are empirically grounded in publicly reported data. For example, the banking system fragility channel (δ1) was modeled with a minimum of 16 billion dollars, a central estimate of 24 billion dollars, and a maximum of 32 billion dollars, reflecting NBER base-case and severe-scenario projections. Similarly, the pension fund wealth destruction channel (δ3) used a range of 23 to 41 billion dollars, anchored by reported write-downs from large public pension funds like the California Public Employees' Retirement System and the California State Teachers' Retirement System. In contrast, the aggregate revenue denominator (Π) was modeled using a uniform distribution, ranging from 11 billion dollars to 15 billion dollars, with a central estimate of 13 billion dollars. This uniform distribution reflects a genuine uncertainty about the precise measurement of private payoff from current institutional arrangements, acknowledging that no single value in this range is inherently more likely than another. The use of different distribution types (triangular for costs, uniform for payoff) is a deliberate choice to capture the specific characteristics of each parameter. To account for the interdependencies between channels, a correlation structure was incorporated into the Monte Carlo simulation. Pairwise correlations were assigned based on documented causal linkages. For instance, a correlation of 0.40 was applied between banking system fragility (C1) and municipal fiscal erosion (C2), reflecting how bank failures depress local commercial real estate values, worsening municipal tax bases. A correlation of 0.50 was used between municipal fiscal erosion (C2) and transit system fiscal collapse (C5), as both are driven by reduced central business district foot traffic and shared funding mechanisms. All other channel pairs were assigned a background macroeconomic correlation of 0.15. This correlation structure elevates the aggregate welfare cost above a simple additive sum, accounting for covariance effects that amplify the total system welfare destruction. The Monte Carlo results yielded a mean βW of 8.38 and a median βW of 8.22. The 90 percent confidence interval for βW was [5.9, 12.1], indicating a robust range for the system beta. The distribution of βW was found to be right-skewed (skewness of 0.62), with the mean exceeding the median. This right skewness is a structural feature, primarily driven by the asymmetric tails of the pension and banking channels, which have more upside risk (potential for larger losses) than downside. The standard deviation of βW was 1.94, indicating moderate parametric uncertainty. Crucially, the simulation showed that the probability of βW falling below 1.0 was 0.0000 percent, unequivocally confirming that current commercial real estate institutional arrangements are net welfare-destroying under any plausible parameter combination. The impossibility floor of βW ≥ 4.7 was never breached in any of the 100,000 draws. Sensitivity analysis, including a tornado chart, identified the pension fund wealth destruction channel (δ3) as the largest contributor to βW variance (31 percent), followed by banking system fragility (δ1) at 24 percent, and the revenue denominator (Π) at 18 percent (due to its denominator effect). Robustness tests across different distribution types (lognormal, normal, triangular) confirmed that the qualitative classification of commercial real estate as a high-beta institutional Private Pareto Theorem domain remained invariant. The classification would only change under extreme scenarios, such as a dramatic expansion of the private payoff definition to include all commercial real estate net operating income, which the paper argues would conflate welfare-neutral activity with welfare-destroying institutional margins. This comprehensive Monte Carlo methodology ensures that the βW estimate and its implications are robust to parameter uncertainty and provide a reliable basis for policy analysis.

caseStudies.title: San Francisco's Urban Doom Loop
caseStudies.content:
San Francisco stands as the quintessential example of the urban doom loop in action, with its commercial real estate crisis manifesting across multiple welfare channels. The city's office vacancy rate has soared to an unprecedented 31 to 37 percent, depending on the measurement, marking the highest level in its recorded history. This dramatic decline in occupancy has triggered a severe municipal fiscal crisis. The city's budget faces a projected 600 to 900 million dollar annual revenue shortfall through 2029, primarily due to the erosion of its commercial property tax base. This shortfall, exacerbated by a three-year assessment lag, forces the city to consider deep cuts to essential public services, including police, sanitation, and transit, or to impose higher taxes on residential property owners.

The degradation of public services and quality of life accelerates the outmigration of businesses and residents, further deepening the commercial property vacancy spiral. The Bay Area Rapid Transit system, a critical component of the region's infrastructure, continues to operate at 35 percent below pre-pandemic ridership levels, contributing to its own fiscal cliff and forcing service reductions. This directly impacts service workers and low-income residents who rely on public transportation. The city's once-vibrant central business district service economy has collapsed, with lost commuter spending contributing to the broader service worker displacement channel.

While San Francisco has declared itself the "Artificial Intelligence Capital of the World" and has seen over 6 million square feet leased by artificial intelligence and biotech firms since 2023, this absorption has been highly selective. It primarily benefits Class A premier office space in tech-adjacent neighborhoods like South of Market and Mid-Market, creating the appearance of recovery. However, the city's overall vacancy rate remains above 30 percent, as Class B and C buildings in the Financial District and peripheral submarkets continue to languish. This hyper-specialized demand absorption, without structural market clearing for the broader commercial real estate stock, functions as a delay mechanism rather than a resolution. It masks the continued deterioration of the majority of the market, allowing system welfare costs to compound in the 80 percent of buildings that artificial intelligence does not touch. The San Francisco case vividly illustrates the non-linear, self-reinforcing nature of the doom loop, where the marginal welfare cost per additional percentage point of vacancy accelerates beyond the 20 percent threshold.

caseStudies.title: Oakland's Fiscal Emergency and the Doom Loop's Acute Impact
caseStudies.content:
Oakland, California, provides a stark illustration of the urban doom loop's acute impact on a city with less fiscal resilience than its larger neighbor, San Francisco. With an office vacancy rate reaching 29.3 percent, Oakland has crossed the 20 percent threshold that activates the self-reinforcing cascade of commercial real estate distress. The city declared a fiscal emergency, facing a substantial 180 million dollar deficit over a two-year period. This deficit has necessitated severe cuts to public services, directly impacting the quality of life for its residents and accelerating the outmigration of businesses and individuals.

The mechanism of welfare destruction in Oakland is a textbook example of municipal fiscal erosion. As commercial property values plummet due to high vacancy, the city's property tax revenues—a critical component of its general fund—decline significantly after the three-year assessment lag. This revenue shortfall directly translates into reduced funding for essential services such as public safety, sanitation, and infrastructure maintenance. These service cuts, in turn, make the city less attractive, further depressing commercial real estate demand and accelerating the outmigration of businesses and residents. This creates a vicious cycle where fiscal distress begets more vacancy, and more vacancy exacerbates fiscal distress.

Oakland's proximity to San Francisco introduces a unique compounding factor: it faces a double doom loop. Not only does it grapple with its own internal commercial real estate crisis, but it also experiences spillover effects from San Francisco's fiscal challenges. Reduced Bay Area Rapid Transit service, a consequence of San Francisco's transit fiscal collapse, directly impacts Oakland commuters and further reduces foot traffic in its central business district. The competition for a shrinking pool of tenants in the broader Bay Area exacerbates Oakland's commercial real estate woes. The city's smaller tax base and fewer alternative revenue sources, compared to San Francisco, make it particularly vulnerable to these cascading effects. The Oakland case demonstrates that for cities with high fiscal fragility, the doom loop can rapidly lead to declared fiscal emergencies and substantial, painful cuts to public services, directly impacting the welfare of its non-consenting residents and service workers.

caseStudies.title: Pension Fund Losses: CalPERS, CalSTRS, and NYCERS
caseStudies.content:
The commercial real estate crisis directly erodes the retirement security of millions of public employees through significant pension fund wealth destruction. Public pension funds across the United States hold approximately 460 billion dollars in real estate allocations, making them highly exposed to the downturn in commercial property values. When these values decline, the funds' unfunded liabilities grow, creating a direct welfare cost that is ultimately borne by taxpayers (through increased employer contributions) or retirees (through reduced benefits).

The California Public Employees' Retirement System, or CalPERS, the largest public pension fund in the United States with 462.8 billion dollars in total assets, reported an 8.1 percent one-year return on its office portfolio. This underperformance significantly contributed to a staggering 30 billion dollar total fund loss in fiscal year 2022. This 8.1 percent loss on office real estate stands in stark contrast to CalPERS' actuarial assumed return of approximately 6.8 percent, meaning the fund is consistently falling short of its targets due to commercial real estate exposure. Each year the real estate portfolio underperforms by such margins, the unfunded liability grows by an estimated 3 to 5 billion dollars, requiring increased contributions from California state and local governments.

Similarly, the California State Teachers' Retirement System, or CalSTRS, with 341 billion dollars in total assets, reported a 9 percent return on its real estate portfolio in 2023. This resulted in a 1.7 billion dollar write-down in a single six-month period. These are not abstract financial figures; they represent a direct reduction in the wealth underpinning the retirement plans of California's public school teachers. The illiquidity premium historically associated with real estate investment, combined with "stale pricing" of private market assets, means that pension boards often see smaller reported volatility, which can mask the true extent of the losses and reinforce continued allocation to the asset class, perpetuating the problem.

Beyond California, the New York City Employees' Retirement System, or NYCERS, with approximately 80 billion dollars in total assets, provides another clear example of this welfare channel. Investment losses, including those from commercial real estate, forced an 866 million dollar increase in unexpected additional funding from the Metropolitan Transportation Authority's budget. This capital was diverted directly from transit operations, degrading New York City's public transportation services. This illustrates a direct fiscal linkage where pension fund losses, driven by commercial real estate distress, impose welfare costs on transit riders—a non-consenting third party. These cases collectively demonstrate that pension fund wealth destruction is a high-probability, high-severity channel, with losses that are certain, ongoing, and compound through unfunded liability growth, directly impacting millions of public sector retirees and taxpayers.

caseStudies.title: Signature Bank's Failure: A Commercial Real Estate Contagion Case Study
caseStudies.content:
The failure of Signature Bank in March 2023 serves as a critical case study illustrating the banking system fragility channel and the contagion risks embedded within the commercial real estate crisis. Signature Bank, with 110 billion dollars in total assets, was one of three major regional banks to collapse in a single quarter, contributing to a 34.2 billion dollar cost to the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund. Its failure was explicitly and profoundly linked to its substantial commercial real estate exposure.

At the time of its collapse, Signature Bank held a 33 billion dollar commercial real estate portfolio. This portfolio was so impaired and toxic that when the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation facilitated the acquisition of Signature Bank's assets by Flagstar Bank, the acquiring institution explicitly refused to take on the commercial real estate portfolio. This unprecedented move forced the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation to retain and manage the entire 33 billion dollar portfolio itself, highlighting the extreme credit risk associated with these assets. The portfolio included 15 billion dollars in New York City multifamily exposure, which, while not office-specific, demonstrated the broader distress across commercial property types.

The subsequent distress experienced by New York Community Bancorp, which eventually absorbed a portion of Signature Bank's commercial real estate portfolio, further underscores the contagion mechanism. New York Community Bancorp faced significant financial pressure and saw its stock price plummet due to concerns over its commercial real estate loan book, which included the acquired Signature Bank assets. This demonstrated how commercial real estate losses at a failed bank can propagate to the acquiring institution, creating a cascade of impairment and broader market instability.

Signature Bank's case provides empirical validation for the mechanisms described in the banking system fragility channel. It illustrates how "extend-and-pretend" practices, by deferring loss recognition, can lead to a sudden and catastrophic failure when market conditions deteriorate. It also highlights the immense exposure of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation's Deposit Insurance Fund to concentrated commercial real estate risk, as the fund is ultimately responsible for absorbing these losses. The bank's failure sent a clear signal to the market about the lethal interaction of commercial real estate credit risk with other financial vulnerabilities, contributing to the broader repricing of commercial real estate-exposed assets and elevating the tail risk of systemic contagion within the banking sector.

caseStudies.title: The WeWork Template: Artificial Occupancy and Valuation Inflation
caseStudies.content:
WeWork's dramatic trajectory from a 47 billion dollar valuation in January 2019 to a 45 million dollar bankruptcy estate in November 2023 serves as a crucial template for understanding how artificial occupancy distorts commercial real estate markets and externalizes losses. At its peak, WeWork was the largest private tenant in New York City, managing approximately 5.3 million square feet, and a top tenant in other major metropolitan areas. Its business model involved signing long-term master leases on entire buildings, subdividing the space into flexible co-working memberships, and capturing the spread, funded by over 22 billion dollars in venture capital and debt.

The welfare-destroying mechanism was the creation of artificial occupancy. By signing master leases at above-market rates, WeWork inflated building occupancy rates, suppressed vacancy statistics, and supported property valuations that would have been significantly lower without this synthetic demand. Landlords booked inflated rental income, using it to support property valuations and refinancing. Banks underwrote loans against WeWork-occupied buildings based on these artificially elevated occupancy and cash flow metrics. Commercial mortgage-backed securities pools that included WeWork-occupied buildings had distorted risk profiles, as the market treated this artificial occupancy as structural rather than contingent.

When WeWork collapsed, this artificial occupancy vanished, transmitting welfare costs through several channels. Landlords faced substantial losses and property value write-downs; Brookfield Asset Management, for instance, wrote down 1.1 billion dollars on its WeWork-related investments, and SL Green Realty took a 500 million dollar impairment. Commercial mortgage-backed securities loans backed by WeWork-occupied properties, some representing 30 to 50 percent of a building's occupancy, deteriorated immediately upon lease rejection, with at least 2 billion dollars in such loans transferred to special servicing since November 2023. The loss severity on these loans was elevated because buildings lost not only the rental stream but also the occupancy metrics supporting broader marketability.

Beyond direct losses, WeWork's collapse sent a market-wide signal that flexible office demand was overestimated and that occupancy statistics based on co-working tenancy were unreliable. This signal contributed to a broader repricing of office commercial real estate risk, amplifying the pre-existing remote work-driven vacancy by making investors more cautious about all forms of non-traditional office tenancy. The annualized non-consenting party loss attributable to WeWork is estimated at 1.3 to 2.3 billion dollars per year, a modest figure in absolute terms but disproportionate relative to WeWork's peak market share, confirming that artificial occupancy is a welfare amplifier. This case demonstrates that any mechanism—whether venture-funded demand, government leases, or corporate return-to-office mandates—that creates synthetic occupancy distorts commercial real estate valuations and externalizes the eventual correction onto non-consenting third parties.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework governing commercial real estate in the United States is a fragmented patchwork of federal, state, and municipal authorities, none of which are adequately equipped to address the systemic welfare costs of the office vacancy crisis. At the federal level, banking regulators—the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, and the Federal Reserve—have primary jurisdiction over banks holding commercial real estate loans. Their authority stems from statutes like the Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery, and Enforcement Act of 1989 and the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010, as well as specific guidance such as 12 C.F.R. Part 365 and the 2006 Interagency Commercial Real Estate Concentration Guidance. These frameworks are designed to ensure bank safety and soundness, but their implementation has consistently permitted "extend-and-pretend" practices, where maturing loans are extended without recognizing underlying losses. On the tax front, federal law includes the 1031 exchange (Section 1031 of the Internal Revenue Code), which allows investors to defer capital gains taxes indefinitely on property sales if proceeds are reinvested in "like-kind" property. This provision, costing the Treasury 10.2 billion dollars per year, acts as a significant disincentive for commercial real estate owners to sell distressed assets at market-clearing prices, thereby locking capital into unproductive holdings. Additionally, the Section 199A pass-through deduction, enacted in the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, provides a 20 percent deduction on qualified business income from pass-through entities, including Real Estate Investment Trust distributions, further subsidizing commercial real estate ownership. At the state level, legislatures govern property tax laws, including assessment cycles and tax reduction factors. Most states have assessment lags, typically three years, which delay the recognition of commercial property value declines in tax rolls. This creates a fiscal cliff for municipalities, as tax revenues are based on outdated valuations. State laws also dictate zoning authority, which is then delegated to municipal governments. Zoning regulations, such as floor area ratios, height limits, and use restrictions, often impede adaptive reuse of vacant office buildings by making conversions to residential or other uses difficult or impossible without lengthy and costly variance processes. Municipal governments, through their planning commissions and city councils, have direct control over zoning and land use. They also manage property tax assessments and collect revenues that fund essential public services. However, their ability to respond to commercial real estate distress is constrained by state laws and their dependence on commercial property tax revenues. Many cities lack the legal authority or financial capacity to implement comprehensive adaptive reuse programs or to force market clearing without state-level support. Collectively, this framework is characterized by fragmentation, with federal banking regulators focused on bank solvency, federal tax law incentivizing asset holding, and state/municipal authorities grappling with property taxation and land use. There is no single, coordinated authority or mechanism designed to address the systemic welfare costs arising from the interaction of these disparate policies. The existing framework is largely reactive, designed to manage individual failures rather than prevent systemic ones, and it is heavily influenced by the commercial real estate lobbying apparatus, which actively works to preserve the very institutional arrangements that generate welfare destruction.
